policy_statement: Prepare operating models and examples for energy-efficient solutions in renovation of protected buildings.
mechanism_description: The city will prepare operating models and good examples to support energy-efficient renovation of protected buildings, helping reduce building energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Same building service (comfort/utility) → ↓ energy input (renovation efficiency) → ↓ fuel/electricity generation → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Building owners/renovators of protected buildings adopt higher-efficiency retrofit practices and operational models (e.g., improved insulation where permissible, airtightness, efficient HVAC/controls, better windows, optimized operation) supported by city guidance and exemplars. (2) Mediating process: Reduced heat losses and improved system efficiencies lower final energy demand for heating/cooling/ventilation and other services. (3) Climate outcome: Lower energy demand reduces upstream emissions from district heat/electricity production and/or direct on-site combustion, yielding net GHG reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption, which reduces GHG emissions from electricity/heat and fuel supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: informational/technical guidance → more energy-efficient renovation choices → reduced energy use for the same building service → reduced GHG emissions from energy supply.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while the ultimate benefit is mitigation (lower emissions), the direct and consistent mechanism across all such policies is reducing energy input per unit service (efficiency), which fits Resource Efficiency as the primary category under the dominant pathway framing.  
3) Edge cases applied: if guidance primarily caused fuel-switching (e.g., electrification/renewables) rather than efficiency, Mitigation could dominate; if guidance focused on overheating resilience or flood-proofing, Adaptation could dominate. Those are not implied here.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy that creates operating models/examples to enable energy-efficient renovations has the same dominant effect—lower building energy demand—regardless of city context or specific protected-building constraints.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: specific emissions impacts (grid intensity, district heat fuel mix), measures encouraged, and any co-benefits (comfort, heritage preservation) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Protected-building renovations can be constrained (limiting efficiency gains) and some guidance could emphasize material reuse/low-carbon materials (embodied carbon) or electrification; if those dominate in a specific policy instance, the primary category might shift toward Mitigation, but the stated mechanism is operational guidance for energy-use reduction.
key_indicators: "Operation models"; "good examples"; "energy-efficient solutions"; "renovation of protected buildings"; "will be prepared"
co_benefits: Improved indoor comfort; lower energy bills; preservation-compatible renovation practices/heritage protection capacity-building
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy/technical guidance instrument; classified as information-disclosure because the action is explicitly to “prepare” models/examples (knowledge products) rather than adopt a plan or mandate implementation.
